detect:
package.json at the resolve root, reached only after the Flutter, React Native and Gradle/Xcode rules have all missed. This is a FAMILY profile: detect_profile() returns node-web for every package.json repo, so it covers Vite, Next, Nuxt, SvelteKit, Remix, an Express service and a plain CLI alike. It therefore cannot assume a framework or a script vocabulary; a repo pins those in its own .claude/factory.json overlay.

MONOREPO GOTCHA: the native markers are checked FIRST, so from a repo root that also holds android/settings.gradle.kts or iosApp/ you will always get the native profile. Run the resolver and the factory from the web subdirectory to select this one.
gatesComment:
SCRIPT NAMES ARE A GUESS AND ARE MEANT TO BE OVERRIDDEN per repo, the same way the KMP profile's `:app` module path is. Read package.json's scripts block and pin the real chain in the repo overlay. A typical stronger chain is `npm run typecheck && npm run test:run && npm run build`: typecheck first because it fails fastest with the clearest message, the build last because it is the only step that catches bundler-level breakage (asset imports, env access, resolution) that the type-checker cannot see.

WATCH MODE WILL HANG THE GATE FOREVER. `vitest`, `jest --watch` and `next dev` never exit. The gate must call a one-shot variant (`vitest run`, `jest --ci`, etc). If package.json only defines a watch-mode `test`, add a `test:run` and use that.

NO `snapshot` GATE IS DEFINED. Most node repos have no visual-regression tooling, and the engine filters GATE_ORDER to the keys actually present, so omitting it is honest rather than broken. Do NOT invent one: a fabricated command that trivially exits 0 reports green while proving nothing. Real options, once the tooling exists, are DOM snapshots (cheap, catches markup drift, blind to CSS) or Playwright screenshots (catches real rendering, needs browsers in CI). Add the tool first, then the gate.

NO `e2e` GATE IS DEFINED either, for the same reason. A repo that has one (Playwright, Cypress, or a cross-client parity check) adds it in its overlay.

gateNotes.fast: An empty test run is a FAILURE, not a pass. Vitest and Jest both exit 0 when a glob matches nothing, so a broken test path looks identical to a green suite. Confirm the output printed a non-zero test count before reporting pass.

conventions:
IDENTIFY THE FRAMEWORK BEFORE WRITING ANYTHING. This profile is deliberately framework-agnostic, so read package.json's dependencies and the existing source, and follow that stack's idioms (routing, data fetching, state, file layout). Do not import React patterns into a Svelte codebase or App Router patterns into a Pages Router one. Match the surrounding code's naming, comment density and idiom; read neighbouring files first.

TypeScript strict where the repo is strict. Do not widen or disable type checking to make a build pass, and do not add `any` or a ts-expect-error to silence a real error.

No new dependencies without strong justification. A node project's dependency tree is its largest long-term liability, and most small utilities are a few lines of local code.

Wire-boundary values keep their transport types. Timestamps that travel as epoch-millis numbers stay numbers until presentation; do not let a Date or a formatted string leak back into the model, and do not parse-then-reserialize in a way that changes types.

reviewDimensions.ask: Logic bugs, edge cases, null/undefined/empty handling, async races and unawaited promises, state that fails to reset between operations, and error paths that swallow failures silently.

Follows the framework actually in use (per package.json) rather than a neighbouring one, module boundaries respected, no needless dependencies, no type-checking widened or suppressed to pass a build, wire-boundary types preserved.

procedures.workdir: Run everything from the directory holding package.json, not from a monorepo root. In a monorepo with a native app the resolver's native rule wins at the root and would hand you the wrong profile and the wrong gates.
procedures.overlay: This is a family profile. Anything specific to one repo (real script names, extra gates, project conventions, framework-specific review dimensions) belongs in that repo's .claude/factory.json overlay, which merges on top: conventions concatenate, gates and procedures merge per key, and reviewDimensions concatenate with the overlay winning on a key clash.
